sections.Introduction: Coronary artery disease (CAD) causes approximately one third of all deaths in persons over 35 years. Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) represents a spectrum — STEMI, NSTEMI, and unstable angina — sharing the common pathogenic mechanism of atherosclerotic plaque rupture and intracoronary thrombus formation. ACS accounts for nearly 2 million hospitalisations annually in the United States; mortality may reach 25% when pre-hospital deaths are included. Management is time-dependent: 'time is muscle.' In the ICU, establishing the type of MI and the cause of troponin elevation is especially important for treatment and prognosis.
sections.Terminology: ACS is classified at initial presentation by ECG: (1) STEMI — benefit from immediate reperfusion; (2) NSTEMI — troponin elevation without STE; fibrinolytics contraindicated; (3) Unstable Angina — ischaemic symptoms, no troponin rise above 99th percentile URL. Fourth Universal Definition MI types: Type 1 — atherothrombotic plaque disruption (DAPT indicated). Type 2 — O2 supply-demand imbalance (spasm, tachyarrhythmia, anaemia, hypotension; DAPT NOT indicated). Type 3 — sudden death before biomarkers obtained. Type 4a — post-PCI, troponin >5× 99th URL within 48h. Type 4b — stent thrombosis. Type 5 — CABG-associated, troponin >10× 99th URL. Myocardial injury: cTn > 99th percentile URL. AMI: rising/falling cTn pattern (≥1 value above URL) plus clinical/ECG evidence of ischaemia. MINOCA: MI with no coronary stenosis ≥50% in a major epicardial vessel. In the ICU, Type 2 MI is more common; elevated troponin from sepsis, RHF, PE, or renal failure must be distinguished from Type 1 MI. Echocardiography detecting RWMA can assist in ICU diagnosis.
sections.Pathophysiology: Vulnerable atherosclerotic plaques (thin fibrous cap, lipid-rich core) rupture, exposing subendothelial matrix. Platelet adhesion and activation form platelet-rich 'white thrombus.' Coagulation cascade activation produces fibrin-red cell 'red thrombus.' Treatment relevance: antiplatelet agents (aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors) target white thrombus; fibrinolytics lyse red thrombus but are NOT active against white thrombus. Total occlusion → STEMI. Partial occlusion → NSTEMI/UA. Complications of AMI: cardiogenic shock (>40% LV loss); papillary muscle rupture → acute MR (apical murmur); VSD (anterior/inferior MI, lower sternal border murmur with thrill); free wall rupture (catastrophic, sudden); RV infarction (↓compliance + ↓systolic function → venous congestion + LOW CO); ventricular remodelling (negative). About 10% of ACS present with STE on initial ECG.
sections.Cardiogenic Shock: CS complicates 7–10% of STEMI and 2–3% of NSTEMI. Median time after STEMI for shock onset: 5–6 hours (often does NOT present at admission). In NSTEMI, CS occurs later: median 76–94 hours. Pathophysiology: ↓contractility → ↓CO → ↓BP → ↑coronary ischaemia → further ↓contractility (vicious cycle). Compensatory vasoconstriction ↑afterload. With time, systemic inflammation (NO, peroxynitrite, TNF, IL) causes pathological vasodilation. CS causes (incidence): LV failure 78.5%, ischaemic MR 6.9%, VSD 3.9%, RV failure 2.8%, tamponade 1.4%. Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required for CS in absence of mechanical complications. High index of suspicion and rapid echocardiography required for diagnosis.
sections.Recognition and ECG: First ECG target: within 10 minutes of first medical contact. Serial ECGs critical (ACS = dynamic changes). ECG evolution: Hyperacute (0–20 min): peaked T-waves, upward ST coving. Acute (mins–hours): STE, Q-wave development. Early (hours–days): Q waves, T-wave inversion. Indeterminate (days–weeks): Q waves, persisting T-inversion. Old (weeks–months): Q waves, normalised ST/T. STEMI STE criteria: ≥1mm in all leads EXCEPT V2–V3. In V2–V3: ≥2mm men >40y; ≥2.5mm men ≤40y; ≥1.5mm women (any age). LBBB + ischaemic symptoms → manage as STEMI regardless of prior ECG availability. Sgarbossa criteria in LBBB: concordant STE ≥1mm; concordant STD ≥1mm V1–V3; discordant STE ≥5mm. Posterior MI: V7–V9 (circumflex occlusion + anterior STD). RV infarction: V3R, V4R in inferior MI. Left main/multivessel: STD ≥1mm in ≥8 surface leads + STE in aVR and/or V1. Non-MI STE causes: Brugada (V1–V3 + RBBB pattern), Takotsubo, pericarditis, LVH, LV aneurysm, PE, SAH, WPW. Takotsubo: post-menopausal female, physical/emotional stress, precordial STE, apical ballooning + basal sparing on echo, normal coronary angiography (1–2% of STEMI). A normal ECG does NOT exclude AMI. Inferior MI + high-degree AV block + RBBB → proximal RCA (in-hospital mortality 2.5× higher).
sections.Biochemical Markers: Myocardial injury: cTn > 99th percentile URL. Acute MI: dynamic rising/falling pattern + ≥1 value above URL + ischaemia evidence. cTnI and cTnT are preferred (highly specific for cardiac myocytes). hs-cTn assays: recommended over conventional assays. cTn kinetics: detection 4–6h post-injury, peak 12–24h, returns to normal 7–14 days. hs-cTn: shortens diagnosis to 1–3h in most patients; sampling at 0h and repeat at 3–6h (earlier with hs-cTn algorithms). Late presenters (>12–18h): troponin may already be falling — a falling pattern alone satisfies AMI definition; absence of significant delta does not exclude acute injury if clinical context is compatible. CK-MB: less sensitive/specific; also present in muscle and brain; onset 4–6h, peaks 18–24h, normalises 48–72h. Best alternative when cTn unavailable. Non-ischaemic causes of cTn elevation: HF, myocarditis, PE, sepsis, renal failure, stroke, SAH, arrhythmias, chemotherapy. Dynamic pattern distinguishes acute from chronic injury. Single value above URL = injury, not necessarily acute MI.
sections.Imaging Techniques: Echocardiography (TTE): technique of choice at bedside. RWMA detectable when >20% transmural thickness affected. Identifies complications: acute MR (papillary rupture), VSD, free wall rupture, pericardial effusion, RV infarction, aortic dissection. Useful alternative/adjunct to PAC for guiding therapy in CS. TOE has increasing role in complicated MI and ICU. CMR: modality of choice for late-presentation MI and subendocardial scarring; differentiates ischaemic from non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy by late gadolinium enhancement pattern. Also assesses myocardial viability. Coronary angiography: gold standard for CAD diagnosis; identifies culprit artery and allows definitive revascularisation. If biomarkers are normal at appropriate time points, acute MI is excluded — imaging criteria take second place. Normal function on echo practically excludes significant MI (small MI cannot be fully excluded).
sections.Risk Scoring Systems: Killip Classification: Class I = no HF; Class II = rales/S3/elevated JVP; Class III = acute pulmonary oedema; Class IV = cardiogenic shock. GRACE Score (8 variables): age, heart rate, systolic BP, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, elevation of cardiac markers, ST-segment deviation. >140 = HIGH risk of in-hospital mortality; 109–140 = intermediate risk. Used to determine need and timing of invasive stratification. TIMI for UA/NSTEMI (7 predictors, 14-day events): age ≥65, ≥3 CHD risk factors, prior coronary stenosis ≥50%, ST deviation, ≥2 anginal events in 24h, elevated biomarkers, aspirin use in prior 7 days. Score 0/1 → 4.7% events; Score 6/7 → 40.9% events. TIMI for STEMI (8 predictors, 30-day mortality): age ≥75 (3pts), age 65–74 (2pts), DM/HTN/angina history (1pt), SBP <100 (3pts), HR >100 (2pts), Killip II–IV (2pts), weight <67kg (1pt), anterior STE or LBBB (1pt), time to reperfusion >4h (1pt). CRUSADE Bleeding Score (NSTEMI): 8 variables — baseline haematocrit, creatinine clearance, female sex, diabetes, PVD, HF signs, systolic BP, heart rate. Range 1–100. Very high risk ≥50 (19.5% major bleeding).
sections.Conclusion: AMI and UA are often clinically indistinguishable at presentation — hence the ACS terminology. About 10% of ACS patients have ST-segment elevation on initial ECG. A normal ECG does NOT exclude AMI. Clinical examination and echocardiography are important to detect complications and exclude alternative diagnoses. STEMI: reperfusion strategy based on time of presentation. NSTEMI/UA: risk stratify with GRACE, TIMI, CRUSADE; timing of invasive strategy guided by risk. In ICU patients: establish MI type before treatment (DAPT only for Type 1); consider echocardiography for RWMA; troponin elevation is common but must be interpreted in clinical context.

questions.question: A 62-year-old patient is on day 2 post-emergency laparotomy. She develops tachycardia at 126/min, blood pressure 86/52 mmHg, and haemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. hs-cTnT rises from 180 to 540 ng/L (99th percentile URL = 14 ng/L). There is no chest pain and no ST changes on ECG. What MI type is MOST likely?
options: A: Type 1 MI (atherothrombotic plaque disruption with intracoronary thrombosis)
B: Type 4a MI (peri-procedural after percutaneous coronary intervention)
C: Type 5 MI (associated with coronary artery bypass grafting)
D: Type 2 MI (myocardial O2 supply-demand imbalance)
E: Type 3 MI (sudden cardiac death before biomarkers obtained)
questions.explanation: Tachycardia, hypotension, and severe anaemia combine to create myocardial O2 supply-demand imbalance — the defining mechanism of Type 2 MI. No clinical evidence of plaque disruption is present. This distinction is critical in critical care: dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is indicated only for Type 1 MI; in Type 2, treatment targets the underlying stressor (haemodynamic instability, anaemia). Type 4a MI requires troponin >5× 99th URL within 48 hours of PCI and is not applicable here.
questions.topic: Type 1 vs 2 MI ICU supply-demand DAPT differentiation

questions.question: A 68-year-old male has 90 minutes of chest pain. ECG shows 2.5mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF, 1.5mm ST depression in V1-V3, and reciprocal ST depression in aVL. Which additional leads should be recorded FIRST?
options: A: Repeat standard 12-lead ECG in 15 minutes
B: V7, V8, V9 (posterior chest wall leads)
C: Continuous 12-lead monitoring without additional leads
D: V3R and V4R (right precordial leads)
E: High lateral augmentation leads only (I, aVL)
questions.explanation: In inferior MI (STE in II, III, aVF), guidelines recommend V3R and V4R to identify concomitant right ventricular infarction — a diagnosis with critical management implications (avoid nitrates and diuretics; RV preload dependent). V7–V9 are indicated for suspected posterior MI in the context of circumflex occlusion and prominent anterior ST depression; with inferior MI the culprit is typically the RCA, making V3R/V4R the priority. In high-degree AV block complicating inferior MI with RV involvement, in-hospital mortality is 2.5 times higher than inferior MI without AV block.
questions.topic: inferior STEMI V3R V4R RV infarction posterior leads priority

questions.question: A 58-year-old presents with 90 minutes of crushing chest pain. ECG shows LBBB with concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in leads with positive QRS complexes. No prior ECG is available. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A: Serial troponins and repeat ECG after 6 hours of monitoring
B: IV fibrinolytic therapy, as PCI is not the preferred strategy in LBBB
C: Urgent primary PCI strategy
D: Repeat ECG in 30 minutes before committing to a reperfusion strategy
E: Bedside echocardiography to confirm regional wall motion abnormality before PCI
questions.explanation: New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms is a STEMI-equivalent indication for primary PCI. The absence of a prior ECG must NOT delay reperfusion when the clinical context is strongly suggestive — the module explicitly states: 'Lack of a previous ECG should not be a reason to withhold such therapy when the clinical setting suggests myocardial infarction.' Concordant STE ≥1mm (first Sgarbossa criterion) further supports acute occlusion. Awaiting echocardiography before treatment introduces dangerous delay. Fibrinolysis is inferior to PCI and indicated only when PCI is unavailable.
questions.topic: LBBB STEMI equivalent primary PCI Sgarbossa ischaemic symptoms

questions.question: An ICU patient has elevated hs-cTnI. Which of the following would NOT be consistent with a diagnosis of Type 2 MI?
options: A: Severe anaemia reducing myocardial oxygen delivery
B: Atherosclerotic plaque disruption with intracoronary thrombosis
C: Sustained tachyarrhythmia causing increased myocardial oxygen demand
D: Sustained hypotension in a patient with underlying three-vessel coronary artery disease
E: Coronary artery spasm reducing myocardial perfusion
questions.explanation: Type 2 MI is defined by myocardial ischaemia due to O2 supply-demand imbalance rather than plaque disruption. Atherosclerotic plaque disruption with thrombosis is the defining mechanism of Type 1 (spontaneous) MI. This distinction drives treatment: DAPT is indicated for Type 1 but not Type 2. Options A, B, C, and E are all recognised Type 2 mechanisms — tachyarrhythmia (increased demand), spasm and hypotension (reduced supply), and anaemia (reduced O2 delivery) — as listed in the Fourth Universal Definition's classification of supply-demand imbalance.
questions.topic: Type 2 MI supply-demand NOT plaque rupture Type 1 distinction

questions.question: A 61-year-old post-menopausal woman is admitted to ICU after a cardiac arrest occurring minutes after news of a bereavement. ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V4. Emergency coronary angiography reveals entirely normal coronary arteries. Bedside echocardiography shows apical ballooning with normal basal segment contractility. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A: Acute anterior STEMI due to coronary vasospasm (Type 2 MI)
B: MINOCA — Type 1 MI without obstructive coronary artery disease
C: Takotsubo (apical ballooning) syndrome
D: Brugada syndrome unmasked by catecholamine surge
E: Acute lymphocytic myocarditis with regional distribution
questions.explanation: Takotsubo syndrome is characterised by precordial ST elevation, apical ballooning with preserved basal contractility on echocardiography, normal coronary angiography, and a strong association with post-menopausal females and emotional or physical stress. It accounts for 1–2% of clinically suspected STEMI. Normal coronary angiography is the confirming finding, not an exclusionary one. MINOCA requires evidence of AMI (cTn rise/fall) without obstructive CAD but does not produce the characteristic apical-basal dissociation seen here. Brugada syndrome shows an RBBB-like STE in V1-V3 but is not triggered by emotional stress in this manner and would not show apical ballooning.
questions.topic: Takotsubo apical ballooning normal angiography post-menopausal stress STEMI mimic

questions.question: A 76-year-old male develops cardiogenic shock 5 hours after anterior STEMI. Echocardiography estimates 46% of LV myocardium as akinetic. No mechanical complications are identified. Which mechanism MOST accounts for the shock?
options: A: Papillary muscle rupture with acute severe mitral regurgitation
B: Cardiac tamponade from haemopericardium
C: Right ventricular infarction with low cardiac output
D: Post-MI ventricular septal defect
E: Extensive LV contractile failure due to ischaemic myonecrosis
questions.explanation: LV failure is the most common cause of cardiogenic shock in AMI, accounting for approximately 78.5% of cases. Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required to precipitate CS in the absence of mechanical complications; this patient has 46% akinesis. Mechanical complications collectively account for <20% of CS (VSD 3.9%, ischaemic MR 6.9%, RV failure 2.8%, tamponade 1.4%), and none were found on echocardiography. Papillary muscle rupture is the nearest miss but would be visible on echocardiography as acute severe MR and is far less common.
questions.topic: cardiogenic shock LV failure 78% 40% myocardium mechanical complications incidence

questions.question: A 63-year-old male presents 19 hours after onset of severe central chest pain. hs-cTnT is 3 850 ng/L at 0h and 3 440 ng/L at 3h (URL = 14 ng/L). ECG shows Q waves in V1–V4 with T-wave inversion and J-point elevation. CK-MB is elevated and declining. What does this troponin pattern MOST indicate?
options: A: Stable chronic myocardial injury — no acute event has occurred
B: Non-cardiac troponin elevation — the magnitude of the delta is too small to be diagnostic
C: Acute MI on the downslope of the time-concentration curve consistent with late presentation
D: Type 2 MI with demand ischaemia — stable falling troponin distinguishes this from Type 1
E: The falling pattern rules out AMI, which requires a rising phase
questions.explanation: The Fourth Universal Definition requires a 'rising and/or falling pattern' — a falling pattern alone satisfies the criteria for acute MI. In late presenters (>12–18 hours), troponin may already have peaked and be declining. The module states: 'absence of significant changes does not rule out acute myocardial injury if the clinical context is compatible.' Here, Q waves and T-wave inversion support evolving infarction. The 'falling pattern rules out AMI' distractor is the classic misconception — the definition explicitly includes a falling-only trajectory for late presentations. Chronic injury would show a persistently stable, non-dynamic elevation over days, not this falling pattern in a late acute presentation.
questions.topic: falling troponin late presentation AMI downslope dynamic pattern diagnosis

questions.question: A 65-year-old male presents with atypical chest discomfort. ECG shows ST elevation of 1.8mm in lead V2 and 1.0mm in lead V3, with no changes in other leads. Which statement BEST describes these findings?
options: A: Meets criteria in V2 only (the ≥1.5mm threshold applies to women, not men over 40)
B: Meets STEMI criteria — immediate reperfusion should be initiated
C: Normal variant — ST elevation <2mm in anterior leads is physiologically normal in all men
D: Borderline — STEMI criteria are met in V3 (≥1mm applies in all leads including V2–V3)
E: Does NOT meet STEMI criteria for a male older than 40 years in leads V2 and V3
questions.explanation: In leads V2–V3, the sex- and age-adjusted threshold for men older than 40 years is ≥2mm (not the standard ≥1mm used in all other leads). Neither 1.8mm in V2 nor 1.0mm in V3 meets this threshold. Initiating reperfusion therapy for sub-threshold STE risks unnecessary harm. The distractor claiming the ≥1.5mm threshold applies to men over 40 confuses the thresholds: ≥1.5mm applies to women of any age in V2–V3, while for men ≤40 years the cut-point is ≥2.5mm. The distractor claiming the standard ≥1mm applies to V2–V3 is incorrect. These patients require serial ECG monitoring and troponin measurement.
questions.topic: STEMI STE V2-V3 men 2mm women 1.5mm age-adjusted

questions.question: Regarding the GRACE risk score for acute coronary syndrome, which of the following is NOT a component?
options: A: Serum creatinine concentration
B: ST-segment deviation on presenting ECG
C: Cardiac arrest at admission
D: Heart rate at presentation
E: Prior aspirin use within the previous 7 days
questions.explanation: The GRACE score comprises 8 variables: age, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, elevation of markers of myocardial injury, and ST-segment deviation. Prior aspirin use within 7 days is a variable in the TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI — a common exam confusion. A GRACE score >140 identifies high risk of in-hospital mortality; 109–140 identifies intermediate risk. Options A, B, C, and E are all genuine GRACE components.
questions.topic: GRACE score components NOT aspirin TIMI confusion 8 variables

questions.question: A 70-year-old with inferior STEMI, treated successfully with primary PCI, develops acute haemodynamic deterioration on day 3. A new, loud holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill is heard at the lower left sternal border. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A: Post-MI ventricular septal defect
B: Acute left ventricular free wall rupture with haemopericardium
C: Severe aortic stenosis decompensated by haemodynamic stress
D: Right ventricular infarction with tricuspid regurgitation
E: Papillary muscle rupture with acute severe mitral regurgitation
questions.explanation: A new holosystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border with a palpable thrill is the pathognomonic finding of a post-MI ventricular septal defect. VSD occurs with both anterior and inferior wall infarction and accounts for approximately 3.9% of cardiogenic shock after AMI. Papillary muscle rupture is the nearest miss: it also causes a new holosystolic murmur, but it is heard best at the apex, radiates to the axilla, and produces acute mitral regurgitation rather than a lower sternal border murmur with thrill. Free wall rupture presents with sudden tamponade physiology typically without a murmur.
questions.topic: VSD post-MI murmur lower sternal border papillary rupture differentiation

questions.question: A 55-year-old female presents with NSTEMI. She has ≥3 CHD risk factors, prior coronary stenosis >50%, ST deviation on admission ECG, and 3 anginal episodes in the prior 24 hours. hs-cTnI is elevated. She has NOT taken aspirin in the prior 7 days. What is her TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI?
questions.explanation: The TIMI UA/NSTEMI score assigns 1 point each for 7 variables: age ≥65y (No = 0), ≥3 CHD risk factors (Yes = 1), prior stenosis ≥50% (Yes = 1), ST deviation (Yes = 1), ≥2 anginal events in 24h (Yes = 1), elevated biomarkers (Yes = 1), aspirin use in prior 7 days (No = 0). Total = 5. A score of 5 corresponds to a 26.2% 14-day event rate (death, MI, or urgent revascularisation). A score of 4 is the common near-miss answer, typically from overlooking the prior coronary stenosis criterion. Aspirin non-use scores 0 points (the variable scores 1 if aspirin WAS taken, reflecting aspirin-refractory ischaemia).
questions.topic: TIMI NSTEMI score calculation 7 predictors 14-day events aspirin criterion

questions.question: Which of the following variables is included in the TIMI risk score for STEMI but NOT in the GRACE score?
options: A: Serum creatinine concentration
B: DM, hypertension, or angina history
C: Heart rate on presentation
D: Cardiac arrest at admission
E: Elevation of cardiac biomarkers
questions.explanation: The TIMI STEMI score includes prior history of diabetes, hypertension, or angina (1 point), along with age categories, SBP <100 (3 points), HR >100 (2 points), Killip class II–IV (2 points), weight <67kg, anterior STE or LBBB, and time to reperfusion >4h. Prior medical history is not in GRACE. Serum creatinine and cardiac arrest at admission are GRACE components. Elevated biomarkers is a TIMI NSTEMI-score item, not a GRACE component. Heart rate is in both GRACE and TIMI STEMI — not a differentiator. The overlap between GRACE and TIMI components is a frequent exam source of confusion.
questions.topic: TIMI STEMI components DM HTN angina history NOT GRACE differentiation

questions.question: A 67-year-old female presents with severe chest pain and haemodynamic compromise (SBP 78 mmHg). ECG shows ST depression ≥1mm in eight surface leads (inferolateral distribution) and ST elevation in aVR and V1. No ST elevation elsewhere. Which culprit territory does this ECG pattern MOST suggest?
options: A: Isolated left circumflex artery occlusion (posterior territory)
B: Left main coronary artery obstruction or severe three-vessel ischaemia
C: Proximal LAD occlusion (anterior STEMI equivalent)
D: Proximal right coronary artery occlusion (inferior territory)
E: First diagonal branch occlusion (anterolateral territory)
questions.explanation: ST depression ≥1mm in ≥8 surface leads combined with ST elevation in aVR and/or V1, especially with haemodynamic compromise, is the ECG signature of left main coronary artery obstruction, left main equivalent, or severe three-vessel ischaemia. This pattern is explicitly listed in guidelines as an atypical sign warranting an immediate PCI strategy. Proximal LAD occlusion typically produces anterior ST elevation rather than diffuse subendocardial depression — the widespread depression with aVR elevation reflects global subendocardial ischaemia. RCA and circumflex occlusions produce territory-specific ECG changes.
questions.topic: left main ECG ST depression aVR elevation multivessel

questions.question: A patient with inferior STEMI and successful primary PCI develops complete heart block and new right bundle branch block during the hospital admission. Which culprit coronary territory does this complication MOST suggest?
options: A: Left main artery with multi-territory ischaemia
B: Left circumflex artery — lateral territory
C: Left anterior descending artery — anterior STEMI territory
D: Posterior descending artery — isolated inferior territory
E: Proximal right coronary artery
questions.explanation: High-degree AV block complicating inferior MI — particularly with bundle branch block involvement — indicates proximal RCA occlusion with extensive ischaemia encompassing the right ventricle and AV nodal artery. The AV node is supplied by the RCA in approximately 90% of people (right-dominant circulation). The module notes that when high-degree AV block occurs in inferior MI, in-hospital mortality is 2.5 times higher than inferior MI without AV block. In contrast, LBBB complicating anterior MI implicates the LAD (extensive anterior damage). New RBBB in inferior MI specifically indicates proximal RCA involvement.
questions.topic: inferior STEMI AV block RBBB proximal RCA mortality 2.5x higher

questions.question: A 70-year-old patient in the ICU with severe pneumonia has hs-cTnI of 820 ng/L at 0h and 825 ng/L at 6h (URL = 14 ng/L). There is no chest pain and the ECG shows only sinus tachycardia. Which interpretation is MOST appropriate?
options: A: No cardiac evaluation is needed as mild troponin elevation is an expected and clinically insignificant finding in sepsis
B: A non-dynamic (stable) elevation is most consistent with chronic myocardial injury; acute MI by current definition requires a rising or falling pattern
C: This pattern is consistent with Type 2 MI since troponin is elevated in the context of sepsis-related demand
D: This pattern confirms acute MI — any elevation above URL with troponin in this range is diagnostic
E: This likely rules out myocardial injury; the values may reflect laboratory interference
questions.explanation: The Fourth Universal Definition requires a 'rising and/or falling pattern' to diagnose acute MI. A completely stable elevation is most consistent with chronic myocardial injury or persistent structural heart disease. This patient has myocardial injury (cTn > 99th URL) but not acute MI by definition. The Type 2 MI distractor is wrong because Type 2 MI still requires a dynamic pattern — a stable value does not meet this criterion regardless of context. The 'no evaluation needed' distractor is dangerous: elevated troponin in the ICU, even when appearing chronic, warrants monitoring, documentation of baseline, and evaluation to exclude treatable causes. Sepsis can cause genuine acute myocardial injury requiring vigilance.
questions.topic: stable troponin ICU chronic injury no dynamic pattern

questions.question: Regarding the classification and terminology of acute coronary syndromes (ACS):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fibrinolytic therapy is indicated in both STEMI and NSTEMI to lyse intracoronary thrombus.

statements.label: B
statements.text: At initial presentation, STEMI and NSTEMI are distinguished primarily by ECG findings rather than troponin levels.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Unstable angina is defined by ischaemic symptoms without troponin elevation above the 99th percentile URL.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Approximately 50% of patients presenting with ACS have ST-segment elevation on initial ECG.

questions.topic: ACS ECG classification fibrinolysis NSTEMI UA troponin STE incidence
questions.explanation: A: False — Fibrinolytics are contraindicated in NSTEMI (active non-occlusive thrombus; risk of harm without benefit). B: True — The ECG-based classification (STE vs no-STE) drives initial treatment decisions at presentation, while troponin confirms myocardial necrosis retrospectively. C: True — UA is the ACS category where troponin remains below the 99th percentile URL. D: False — Only about 10% of ACS presentations involve ST-segment elevation, not 50%.

questions.question: Regarding the clinical classification of myocardial infarction (Fourth Universal Definition):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is recommended for both Type 1 and Type 2 MI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Type 5 MI (CABG-associated) uses the same biomarker threshold as Type 4a MI.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Type 2 MI results from myocardial oxygen supply-demand imbalance rather than atherosclerotic plaque disruption.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Type 4a MI is defined by troponin elevation >5× the 99th percentile URL within 48 hours of percutaneous coronary intervention.

questions.topic: MI types DAPT Type 1 only 4a 5x CABG 10x
questions.explanation: A: False — DAPT is indicated only for Type 1 MI (atherothrombotic). Type 2 MI should be managed by addressing the underlying supply-demand stressor, not with DAPT. B: False — Type 5 (CABG-associated) MI requires troponin >10× the 99th percentile URL — twice the Type 4a threshold (>5×), not the same threshold. C: True — Type 2 MI is defined by ischaemia due to O2 supply-demand imbalance, not plaque disruption. D: True — Type 4a MI is defined by troponin elevation >5× the 99th percentile URL within 48 hours of PCI.

questions.question: Regarding cardiac troponin kinetics and measurement in suspected AMI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: High-sensitivity cTn assays can shorten the diagnostic window to 1–3 hours in many patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: CK-MB is a more sensitive and specific biomarker for myocardial injury than cardiac troponin.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A falling troponin pattern in a patient presenting 19 hours after symptom onset is consistent with acute MI.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A single hs-cTn value above the 99th percentile URL is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis of acute MI without further sampling.

questions.topic: hs-cTn diagnostic window CK-MB inferior troponin single value insufficient
questions.explanation: A: True — hs-cTn assays reduce time to diagnosis to within 1–3 hours in many patients. B: False — Cardiac troponin (cTnI and cTnT) is both more sensitive and more specific for myocardial injury than CK-MB; CK-MB is also found in skeletal muscle and brain, reducing its specificity. C: True — Late presenters (>12–18h) may already be on the downslope; a falling pattern alone satisfies the AMI definition. D: False — AMI requires a rising and/or falling pattern — a single elevated value indicates myocardial injury but does not confirm acute MI by the Fourth Universal Definition.

questions.question: Regarding ECG interpretation in suspected AMI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In leads V2–V3, the STEMI threshold for men older than 40 years is ≥2mm ST elevation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The first ECG in suspected ACS should be obtained within 10 minutes of first medical contact.

statements.label: C
statements.text: New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms warrants a primary PCI strategy, regardless of prior ECG availability.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A normal 12-lead ECG reliably excludes the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction.

questions.topic: ECG STEMI criteria normal ECG not exclude LBBB minutes
questions.explanation: A: True — V2–V3 use sex- and age-adjusted thresholds (≥2mm men >40y; ≥2.5mm men ≤40y; ≥1.5mm women). B: True — This is the guideline-specified maximum time target from first medical contact to ECG acquisition. C: True — Prior ECG unavailability must not delay reperfusion when new or presumed new LBBB accompanies ischaemic symptoms. D: False — 'A normal ECG does not exclude AMI' — some infarcts (especially posterior and RCA territory) can produce minimal or no STE on a standard 12-lead. Serial ECGs and supplemental leads are essential when clinical suspicion is high.

questions.question: Regarding ECG localisation of AMI and supplemental lead recordings:

statements.label: A
statements.text: ST elevation ≥0.5mm in posterior leads V7–V9 is diagnostic of posterior (inferobasal) MI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Right precordial leads V3R and V4R should be recorded in patients with anterior MI to identify right ventricular infarction

statements.label: C
statements.text: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF is most commonly associated with right coronary artery (RCA) occlusion.

statements.label: D
statements.text: ST depression ≥1mm in ≥8 surface leads with ST elevation in aVR identifies an isolated proximal LAD occlusion.

questions.topic: ECG localisation RCA inferior posterior left main aVR V4R
questions.explanation: A: True — Posterior leads (V7-V9) detect inferobasal infarction not visible on standard leads. B: False — V3R/V4R are recorded for INFERIOR MI (RCA territory, where RV infarction commonly co-occurs), not anterior MI — anterior MI is an LAD-territory event unrelated to RV perfusion. C: True — Inferior leads (II, III, aVF) ST elevation localises to the RCA territory per standard ECG localisation. D: False — This specific ECG pattern (widespread inferolateral ST depression plus aVR/V1 elevation) identifies left main coronary artery obstruction, left main equivalent, or severe three-vessel ischaemia — not an isolated LAD occlusion, which typically produces anterior ST elevation instead.

questions.question: Regarding Takotsubo (apical ballooning) syndrome:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Takotsubo disproportionately affects post-menopausal females and is often triggered by physical or emotional stress.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Finding normal coronary arteries on angiography effectively excludes the diagnosis of Takotsubo.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The echocardiographic hallmark is apical wall motion abnormality with preserved basal segment contractility.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Takotsubo accounts for approximately 10–15% of clinically suspected STEMI

questions.topic: Takotsubo apical ballooning normal angiography confirms post-menopausal incidence STEMI
questions.explanation: A: True — Takotsubo disproportionately affects post-menopausal females and is often triggered by physical or emotional stress. B: False — Normal coronary angiography is the confirming diagnostic finding in Takotsubo, not an exclusionary one — the diagnosis requires precordial STE, apical ballooning with basal sparing, AND absence of obstructive CAD. C: True — The echocardiographic hallmark is apical wall motion abnormality (ballooning) with preserved basal segment contractility. D: False — Takotsubo accounts for approximately 1–2% of clinically suspected STEMI, not 10–15% — a significant overestimate.

questions.question: Regarding cardiogenic shock (CS) complicating acute MI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In STEMI patients, cardiogenic shock most commonly presents at the time of hospital admission.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Cardiogenic shock complicates approximately 7–10% of STEMI cases.

statements.label: C
statements.text: LV failure is the most common underlying cause of CS in AMI, accounting for approximately 78% of cases.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required for CS in the absence of mechanical complications.

questions.topic: cardiogenic shock STEMI incidence timing NOT admission LV 40%
questions.explanation: A: False — The median time from STEMI to the onset of cardiogenic shock is 5–6 hours after admission — shock often develops after initial presentation, not at the time of admission itself. B: True — Cardiogenic shock complicates approximately 7–10% of STEMI cases. C: True — LV failure is the most common underlying cause of CS in AMI, accounting for approximately 78% of cases. D: True — Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required for CS in the absence of mechanical complications.

questions.question: Regarding mechanical complications of acute MI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Papillary muscle rupture typically presents with a new apical holosystolic murmur due to acute mitral regurgitation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Acute LV free wall rupture is characterised by gradual haemodynamic deterioration over 12–24 hours.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Post-MI ventricular septal defect presents with a new holosystolic murmur at the apex, often with a palpable thrill, indistinguishable from papillary muscle rupture

statements.label: D
statements.text: Right ventricular infarction causes decreased cardiac output rather than a hyperdynamic state.

questions.topic: papillary rupture VSD free wall RV infarction low CO
questions.explanation: A: True — Papillary muscle rupture is heard best at the apex, radiating to the axilla, reflecting acute mitral regurgitation. B: False — LV free wall rupture is a catastrophic event presenting with sudden, severe haemodynamic collapse and tamponade physiology — not gradual deterioration — requiring immediate surgical intervention. C: False — Post-MI VSD produces its holosystolic murmur (with thrill) at the LOWER LEFT STERNAL BORDER, not the apex — the apex/sternal-border distinction is exactly what differentiates it from papillary muscle rupture at the bedside, not a similarity between them. D: True — RV infarction causes decreased RV compliance and systolic dysfunction, leading to venous congestion and LOW cardiac output, the opposite of a hyperdynamic state.

questions.question: Regarding risk stratification scoring systems in ACS:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI predicts 14-day risk of death, MI, or urgent revascularisation using 5 independent predictors

statements.label: B
statements.text: A GRACE score >140 identifies patients at intermediate (not high) risk of in-hospital mortality.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The CRUSADE bleeding score includes male sex as one of its 8 variables for NSTEMI in-hospital bleeding risk

statements.label: D
statements.text: The GRACE score includes age, heart rate, systolic BP, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest, biomarker elevation, and ST deviation as its 8 components.

questions.topic: GRACE 140 high risk TIMI NSTEMI CRUSADE female bleeding
questions.explanation: A: False — The TIMI UA/NSTEMI score uses 7 independent predictors, not 5 (age ≥65, ≥3 CHD risk factors, prior stenosis ≥50%, ST deviation, ≥2 anginal events/24h, elevated biomarkers, prior aspirin use). B: False — A GRACE score >140 identifies HIGH risk of in-hospital mortality (not intermediate); 109–140 indicates intermediate risk. C: False — The CRUSADE score includes FEMALE sex (not male sex) as one of its 8 bleeding-risk variables, alongside baseline haematocrit, creatinine clearance, diabetes, PVD, HF signs, systolic BP, and heart rate. D: True — The GRACE score comprises these 8 variables: age, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, elevation of markers of myocardial injury, and ST-segment deviation.

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology of thrombus formation in ACS and the pharmacological implications:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Total coronary artery occlusion by thrombus typically produces ST-segment depression on ECG, while partial occlusion produces ST-segment elevation

statements.label: B
statements.text: Platelet aggregation on ruptured plaque forms platelet-rich 'white thrombus,' which is then reinforced by fibrin and red cells forming 'red thrombus.'

statements.label: C
statements.text: Nitric oxide and pro-inflammatory cytokines contribute to enhanced vasoconstriction, worsening afterload in prolonged cardiogenic shock

statements.label: D
statements.text: Fibrinolytic agents are effective against platelet-rich 'white thrombus' but NOT against fibrin-rich 'red thrombus.'

questions.topic: white red thrombus fibrinolytics NOT antiplatelet NO cardiogenic shock vasodilation
questions.explanation: A: False — Total occlusion typically produces ST-segment ELEVATION (STEMI), while partial/non-occlusive thrombus produces ST depression or no elevation (NSTEMI/UA) — not the reverse. B: True — Platelet aggregation on ruptured plaque forms platelet-rich 'white thrombus,' subsequently reinforced by fibrin and red cells forming 'red thrombus.' C: False — Endothelial/inducible NO synthase and pro-inflammatory cytokines (peroxynitrite, interleukins, TNF) contribute to pathological VASODILATION (not vasoconstriction) as cardiogenic shock progresses, counterbalancing the earlier compensatory vasoconstriction phase. D: False — The relationship is reversed from this statement: fibrinolytic agents lyse fibrin-rich 'red thrombus' but are NOT active against platelet-rich 'white thrombus' — antiplatelet agents target white thrombus instead.

questions.question: A patient's ECG is recorded 3 months after an untreated myocardial infarction. Based on the evolutionary timeline shown in the figure, which panel best represents the expected chronic ECG appearance?
options: A: Panel B (Hyperacute) — tall, peaked T-waves remain the dominant finding
B: Panel A (Normal) — no residual ECG changes are expected this long after infarction
C: Panel C (Acute) — persisting ST-segment elevation with ongoing T-wave inversion
D: Panel D (Indeterminate) — active dynamic ST-segment changes are still expected at 3 months
E: Panel E (Old) — persisting pathological Q-waves with normalised ST-segments and T-waves
questions.explanation: Three months after an untreated infarct falls within the 'weeks to months' timeframe of the Old stage: persisting pathological Q-waves with normalised ST-segments and T-waves, reflecting a stable, fully evolved scar. The Normal panel shows no infarct changes at all. The Hyperacute and Acute panels reflect the first minutes to hours, with active ST-segment/T-wave changes that would have long since resolved by 3 months. The Indeterminate panel (days to weeks) still shows persisting T-wave inversion, which would be expected to have resolved by the Old stage.
questions.topic: ECG evolution stages hyperacute acute early indeterminate old timeline Q-wave

questions.question: The figure shows a 12-lead ECG obtained in a patient with acute chest pain. Based on the pattern of ST-segment changes shown, which coronary territory is most likely affected?
options: A: Inferior territory — RCA or posterolateral branch of the circumflex
B: Anterolateral territory — proximal LAD involving diagonal branches
C: Anterior territory — proximal LAD occlusion
D: Isolated right ventricular territory without inferior involvement
E: Lateral territory — isolated circumflex occlusion
questions.explanation: The ECG shows ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF), the characteristic pattern of inferior wall infarction, most commonly due to occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA) or, less commonly, the posterolateral branch of the circumflex artery. Anterior and anterolateral patterns (LAD territory) would show ST elevation in the precordial leads (V2-V4) and lateral patterns in I, aVL, V5-V6, none of which are the dominant finding here. Right precordial leads (V3R-V4R) should be recorded next to assess for concomitant right ventricular infarction, a recognised complication of proximal RCA occlusion.
questions.topic: inferior MI ECG recognition RCA localisation ST elevation II III aVF

questions.question: A patient presents with acute chest pain. The limb leads of the 12-lead ECG are shown in the figure. Which of the following best describes the QRS morphology demonstrated?
options: A: Normal sinus rhythm with a normal QRS duration and morphology
B: Right bundle branch block — broad QRS with a characteristic rSR' pattern in right precordial leads
C: Complete heart block with atrioventricular dissociation
D: Left bundle branch block — broad QRS with an abnormal depolarisation sequence
E: Ventricular pre-excitation (Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern) with a delta wave
questions.explanation: The limb leads show broad QRS complexes with the characteristic left bundle branch block morphology (broad, often notched R-wave in lead I, deep S-wave in the inferior leads), reflecting delayed left ventricular depolarisation via the right bundle. New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms is managed as a STEMI-equivalent, since the lack of a prior ECG for comparison should not delay reperfusion therapy when the clinical context is strongly suggestive of infarction. RBBB produces a different morphology (rSR' pattern, best seen in right precordial leads not shown here), WPW shows a delta wave with a short PR interval, and complete heart block shows dissociated P-waves and QRS complexes — none of which match this tracing.
questions.topic: LBBB morphology recognition ECG limb leads broad QRS bundle branch block

questions.question: The figure shows the time course of several biochemical markers following acute myocardial infarction, expressed as multiples of the upper limit of normal. A patient develops recurrent chest pain and ECG changes 5 days after an initial MI. Based on the curves shown, which marker would be MOST useful for confirming re-infarction at this time point, given it should have already returned towards baseline from the index event?
options: A: Troponin T, since it remains markedly elevated through day 7 and cannot distinguish new from ongoing injury
B: Troponin I, since it remains elevated throughout the first week for the same reason as troponin T
C: CK-MB, since it has typically returned to near-baseline by day 3-4, making a new rise more specific for re-infarction
D: LDH, since it is the most cardiac-specific of the markers shown
E: AST, since it remains the most persistently elevated marker throughout the week shown
questions.explanation: CK-MB returns to near-baseline within approximately 2-3 days (by the time-course shown), in contrast to troponin T and troponin I, which remain elevated for up to a week or more after the index event. This more rapid resolution makes a new rise in CK-MB more specific for detecting reinfarction occurring several days after an initial MI, since persistently-elevated troponin cannot easily distinguish ongoing/resolving injury from a new event. AST is less cardiac-specific (also present in liver and skeletal muscle) and is not used for this purpose in modern practice. LDH remains elevated for a similarly prolonged period to troponin and lacks cardiac specificity.
questions.topic: biomarker kinetics CK-MB troponin reinfarction detection curve comparison timing

questions.question: A patient presents with acute chest pain. Serial high-sensitivity troponin measurements at 0h and 3h both remain within the normal reference range, and the ECG shows no ischaemic changes. Bedside echocardiography is performed. Which statement best describes the correct interpretation of this combination of findings?
options: A: Echocardiography and troponin measurement carry equal diagnostic weight and neither takes precedence over the other
B: Normal serial troponin measurements at appropriately timed intervals exclude acute MI and take precedence over echocardiographic findings
C: A small regional wall motion abnormality on echocardiography would override normal troponin results and mandate a diagnosis of acute MI
D: Echocardiography should be performed before biomarkers are drawn, since imaging is more sensitive for early infarction
E: A normal echocardiogram with normal wall motion is required to definitively exclude acute MI, regardless of the biomarker results
questions.explanation: When cardiac biomarkers have been sampled at appropriately timed intervals and remain normal, this excludes acute MI and takes precedence over echocardiographic findings — biomarker evidence is the primary diagnostic criterion in the Fourth Universal Definition. Echocardiography's strength lies in detecting regional wall motion abnormalities and mechanical complications, and in excluding alternative diagnoses (aortic dissection, pericardial effusion, severe aortic stenosis), but a small MI can escape detection by echocardiography even though normal biomarkers reliably exclude significant infarction. Echocardiography is not required before biomarker sampling, and an isolated wall motion abnormality without appropriately-timed biomarker confirmation would not override normal serial troponin results.
questions.topic: echocardiography troponin precedence normal biomarkers exclude MI imaging criteria
